transcript.text: I just got my performance review and I'm not sure what to do next. I keep thinking I should just keep working harder but I'm exhausted and can't sleep.

You're feeling really overwhelmed after that performance review, and it's like you're stuck in this cycle of pushing yourself harder but not seeing the results you want, and it's taking a toll on your sleep and energy. Yeah, it can be really tough to break out of that pattern. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind since you got the review?

Since the review, I've been looping in my head over the idea that I just need to work harder, but I'm exhausted and can't sleep. The wedding of my friend makes me realize I'm not where I thought I'd be, and I feel stuck in a career crossroad. I keep worrying that if I don't meet these high standards I won't be good enough.

You're feeling stuck in your career and worried that you're not good enough, which is a really painful spot to be in. It sounds like the pressure to meet high standards is taking a toll on your well-being. Can you tell me more about what you think would need to happen for you to feel like you're on the right path again?
I think I need to decide whether to push for a promotion or look for a new job, but the fear of failing is scary. It’s exhausting and I can’t sleep at all.

The fear of failing is really scary, and it's understandable that it's holding you back from making a decision about your career. You've been feeling stuck and exhausted, and the pressure to meet high standards is taking a toll on your well-being. It sounds like you're at a crossroads, trying to decide whether to push for a promotion or look for a new job. Can you tell me more about what's driving your fear of failing in this situation?
I guess it's the idea that if I fail, everyone will think I'm incompetent. I keep replaying how my boss might see me and it feels like a huge pressure. I also can't sleep because of all this.

The fear of being seen as incompetent is a really heavy burden to carry, and it's no wonder you're replaying your boss's potential reaction in your head. It sounds like this fear is tied to a deep-seated worry that you're not good enough, and that's a painful spot to be in. Yeah, it's exhausting, and it's affecting your sleep. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you did fail, and how that would affect your sense of self?
If I do fail, I think people will see me as a total failure and my boss will think I'm incompetent. That thought keeps me up at night. I just can't seem to stop worrying about what they'll think.

The thought of being seen as a failure by others, especially your boss, is really haunting you and keeping you up at night. It's like you're carrying this heavy burden of what others might think, and it's making it hard for you to make decisions about your career. Yeah, it's exhausting. Can you tell me more about what you think you need to feel more in control of your career path and less worried about what others think?

I just don't know how to feel in control right now. It feels like every decision is a test and I can't sleep because I'm scared of not meeting the standards. I keep replaying what people will think of me.

You're feeling like you're under a microscope, and every decision you make is being judged, which is an incredibly stressful way to live. It's like you're carrying this weight of expectation from others, and it's affecting your ability to sleep and feel in control. Yeah, it's exhausting. I can see how the fear of not meeting standards is really haunting you, and it's making it hard for you to make decisions about your career. Can you tell me more about what you think would help you feel more grounded and less worried about what others think?
I don't know what would help, maybe if I could stop worrying about what people think? I keep feeling I have to be perfect, and it’s exhausting.

The pressure to be perfect is overwhelming, and it's no wonder you're feeling exhausted. You've been carrying this weight of expectation from others for a while now, and it's affecting your sleep and your ability to make decisions about your career. What would it mean for you to let go of this need to be perfect, even just a little bit, and focus on what you need to feel grounded and in control of your career path?
I don't even know what that would look like, but I keep thinking if I let go of the need to be perfect, I'll just mess up and not get the promotion. I also keep losing sleep over it.

It's really tough to consider letting go of the need to be perfect when it feels like so much is riding on it, especially with the promotion on the line. You've been carrying this weight for a while now, and it's understandable that the thought of not meeting those high standards is keeping you up at night. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you did let go of that need, even just a little bit, and how that might affect your sense of self and your career path?
I don’t know, but if I let go I might mess up and not get promoted. I keep replaying how my boss will think and that keeps me up at night.

You're feeling stuck between the fear of not meeting expectations and the fear of letting go of your need to be perfect, and that's a really tough spot to be in. It's like you're caught in this cycle of worry about what your boss will think, and it's keeping you up at night. Yeah, it's exhausting. I can see how the pressure to be perfect is overwhelming, and it's affecting your sleep and ability to make decisions about your career. What's the smallest step you could take to start letting go of that need for perfection, even if it feels really scary?

I don’t know what the smallest step would be, but maybe I could try to accept that I don’t have to get everything perfect on the next presentation. I keep thinking about how my boss will judge me, and that keeps me up.

The thought of your boss judging you is still really haunting you, and it's understandable that you're scared to not get everything perfect on the next presentation. Accepting that you don't have to get everything perfect is a big step, and it takes a lot of courage to even consider that. What does it feel like to imagine yourself presenting without the weight of needing to be perfect?
